params.description: Vertical picture graph appears on left. Books Read This Week data. Scale of 2: each star = 2 books. Monday=10 (5 stars), Tuesday=6 (3 stars), Wednesday=8 (4 stars), Thursday=4 (2 stars), Friday=12 (6 stars).

Vertical picture graph appears on right. Same Books Read This Week data. Scale of 5: each star = 5 books. Monday=10 (2 stars), Tuesday=6 (2 stars, partial), Wednesday=8 (2 stars, partial), Thursday=4 (1 star, partial), Friday=12 (3 stars, partial). Key visible.
Key highlights on scale-5 graph: Each star = 5.

beats.text: In Warmup, you saw something important. Same data, but the scale-of-5 graph needed fewer symbols. That's because each symbol represents MORE.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph on left. Books Read This Week data. Scale of 2.
Vertical picture graph on right. Same data. Scale of 5. Key highlighted.

beats.text: Same data, fewer symbols. That's why scale of 5 is useful when numbers get bigger. What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?
What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?

validator.description: Student selected 5, correct

beats.text: Right. Each symbol represents 5 items. That's the scale.

validator.description: Student selected 2, confusing scale-of-5 with scale-of-2

params.description: Key on scale-5 graph pulses.

beats.text: Look at the key on the right graph. What number does it show?

validator.description: Student selected 10, possibly adding scale values

params.description: Key on scale-5 graph highlights.

beats.text: The key shows what ONE symbol represents. Look at the key. What does it say?

validator.description: Student selected 1, scale-of-1 misconception

params.description: Key on scale-5 graph highlights.

beats.text: The key tells us the scale. Read the key on the right graph. Each symbol represents how many?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Key animates with annotation: Each star = 5.

beats.text: The key shows the scale. It says each star equals 5. That means every symbol on this graph represents 5 books. Scale of 5 means each symbol stands for 5.

beats.text: It's easier to read this picture graph if you can count by 5s. That's exactly what we do with scale of 5.

beats.text: Let's check: does the picture graph match our data table? 4 symbols, scale of 5. Count by 5s with me. 4 symbols, counting by 5s. What's the total?

validator.description: Student selected 20, correct answer, skip-counted by 5s

beats.text: That's it. 20 items. 5, 10, 15, 20. Skip-counting tells you the total in the picture graph and that matches the value in the data table.

params.description: Key highlights: Each ⭐ = 5.

beats.text: You counted the symbols. But look at the key. Each star stands for 5 items, not 1. Count by 5s: one star is 5, two stars is 10. Keep going.

params.description: Swings column highlights showing 4 symbols.

beats.text: Count the symbols in the Swings column. There are 4 symbols. Each symbol represents 5. Skip-count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, and then what?

beats.text: Let me show you. Look at the Swings column. There are 4 symbols, and each symbol equals 5. Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. The total is 20. That's how skip-counting by 5s works with scaled picture graphs.
